Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar May-25-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. LINDA P. LAM & ATTY. MICHAEL L. SCHRAG OF THE GIBBS LAW GROUP, TEL. NO. 1-510-350-9722 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. PATRICIA H. PERRY OF THE BRADLEY CURLEY BARRABEE & KOWALSKI, TEL. NO. 1-415-464-8888 EXT. 286 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT LATCH SO & HIPP CHIROPRACTIC
ATTY. MARK S. ASHWORTH OF ASHWORTH & ASHWORTH LLP, TEL. NO. 1-949-752-9401 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT ARSMTRONG.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Plaintiff Prasanthi Rathinasamy's motion to compel arbitration is granted. All proceedings in this case are stayed pending the completion of the arbitration. Defendant Latch, So & Hipp Chiropractic fails to show that Ms. Rathinasamy waived her right to arbitrate. There is a public policy in favor of arbitration and a waiver will not be lightly inferred. The party claiming a waiver "bears a heavy burden of proof." (Saint Agnes Med. Ctr. v. PacifiCare of California (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1187, 1195.) "[T]he mere filing of a lawsuit does not constitute a waiver of the right to arbitrate." (Doers v. Golden Gate Bridge Dist. (1979) 23 Cal.3d 180, 183.) It appears that Ms. Rathinasamy filed this lawsuit as a protective measure against a statute of limitations defense and because it was unclear whether the defendants would respond meaningfully to Ms. Rathinasamy's arbitration demand. Christensen v. Dewor Developments (1983) 33 Cal.3d 778 is distinguishable. In Christensen the plaintiff acted in bad faith by filing the civil action to obtain information about the defendants' legal strategies. There is no similar bad faith evidence in this case. Nor has Latch shown that it has been prejudiced by the filing of this case. Latch's incurrence of fees and costs is not sufficient to establish prejudice per Saint Agnes. Dr. Armstrong is also obligated to arbitrate this dispute as Latch's ostensible agent. The arbitration agreement provides that "any dispute" and "all claims for monetary damages'against the physician, and the physician's'agents" must be arbitrated. Dr. Armstrong voluntarily accepted the benefits of working at the Latch chiropractic center and the conduct of Latch and Dr. Armstrong reasonably led Ms. Rathinasamy to believe that he was an employee or agent of Latch. (Rathinasamy Dec., pars. 1-4; Ermonian v. Desert Hosp. (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 475, 505.) A nonsignatory to the arbitration agreement such as Dr. Armstrong is bound by that agreement in these circumstances. (Harris v. Sup. Crt. (1986) 188 Cal.App.3d 475.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR # 8189 =302/hk
